---
title: "Version Ranges"
tagline: "Or what to Do When Gradle Chooses a Version You Didn’t Intend"
type: scenario
tool: gradle
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When Gradle Chooses a Version You Didn’t Intend*
This scenario demonstrates Gradle's support for version ranges.

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-a:1.+]
```



```{.groovy include="build.gradle" snippet="s9-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
Resolve the latest version matching the range (e.g., `1.+`).

#### ! Why
Gradle supports Maven-style ranges and its own dynamic version syntax like `1.+`. It queries repository metadata for the highest match, making resolution dependent on current repository state. This is why the same graph behaves differently across tools.

#### ! Scales
Dynamic versions and ranges are the primary cause of "it worked yesterday" failures. If a new version of a library is published to Maven Central between your local build and your CI build, the two builds may resolve different versions. This is why Gradle provides built-in dependency locking (GS11) --- it's specifically designed to tame dynamic versions. In Java/JVM ecosystems, ranges are less common than in npm/pip, precisely because the JVM community has historically valued build reproducibility. Most teams either ban dynamic versions entirely or pair them with strict locking.

#### ! Risk
* LOW Use ranges for `testImplementation`; allows latest test libraries without affecting production.
* MEDIUM Use ranges for internal libraries under strict semver.
* HIGH Use ranges for external libraries; this causes non-reproducible "it worked yesterday" failures.

#### ! Check
**Fast check:** `./gradlew dependencyInsight --dependency <id>`

**What to look for:** The resolved version and the reason.

**Conclusion:** Ranges are dynamic and require locking for determinism.

#### ! Takeaway
Ranges work but are dynamic.

#### Related Scenarios
*   [GS11: Locking](../scenario-11-locking/guide.qmd)

#### ! Wild

A well-known failure mode with dynamic versions occurs when CI resolves a newly published dependency that developer machines have not yet seen. Gradle caches dynamic version results for [24 hours by default](https://docs.gradle.org/current/userguide/dynamic_versions.html), controlled by `cacheDynamicVersionsFor`. A developer's Gradle cache holds yesterday's resolution; CI, running with `--refresh-dependencies` or a fresh cache, picks up the new version. The build fails without any source changes. Gradle's [dependency locking](https://docs.gradle.org/current/userguide/dependency_locking.html) feature was introduced specifically to make dynamic version resolution reproducible across environments.
